items.title: Dynamic report design separates template, query, and presentation layers
items.description: A dynamic report is composed from three independently versioned artifacts: a data definition (the parameterized query or dataset binding), a template (layout, columns, grouping, formatting, and computed fields), and a render target (HTML, PDF, XLSX, CSV). Keeping these layers decoupled lets a report builder swap a render target or edit a layout without re-authoring the query, and lets the same dataset feed multiple templates. Computed/derived fields belong in the template layer expressed over named dataset columns, never as ad-hoc string concatenation into SQL. The report engine resolves a report instance at run time by binding parameter values to the dataset, executing it, then projecting the result through the selected template version.
items.applicability: Applies to any report builder that allows non-developers to assemble or modify reports against curated datasets.
items.example: A 'Daily Reconciliation' report references dataset DS_RECON (a stored, reviewed query), template T_RECON_v4 (groups by branch, totals amount, formats currency), and target=PDF. Changing the layout to add a subtotal row produces T_RECON_v5 while DS_RECON is untouched.
items.limitation: Strict layer separation adds indirection and a resolver step; very simple one-off exports may not justify the three-artifact model.

items.title: Editable report templates use a constrained, allow-listed expression model
items.description: User-editable templates must express layout and computed fields through a constrained domain model (named columns, an allow-listed function set, and declarative formatting), not through arbitrary code or raw SQL fragments. Templates are stored as structured documents (JSON or a typed DTO), validated on save against the bound dataset's column schema, and rejected if they reference unknown columns or disallowed functions. This prevents template authors from injecting expressions that bypass row-level security or exfiltrate data, and it makes templates portable across render targets. Treat saved templates as data, never eval them as code.
items.applicability: Applies wherever business users edit report layout, columns, grouping, or formulas through a UI.
items.example: Template formula `SUM([net_amount])` and `FORMAT([txn_date], 'yyyy-MM-dd')` validate against allowed functions; `SUM((SELECT ...))` or `System.IO.File.ReadAllText(...)` is rejected at save with a schema-validation error.
items.limitation: An allow-listed expression model cannot cover every power-user need; complex transforms must be promoted into the reviewed dataset layer instead.

items.title: Report parameters are typed, validated, and bound — never string-concatenated
items.description: Every report parameter carries a declared type (date, date-range, int, decimal, enum, multi-select, string), a required/optional flag, an optional default, and validation rules (min/max, regex, allowed values). Parameter values must reach the data layer through bound query parameters (`@p`) or an ORM parameter object, never by string interpolation into SQL, which is the primary SQL-injection vector in reporting. Free-text parameters are length-bounded and pattern-validated; enum and lookup parameters are constrained server-side to their allowed set so a tampered request cannot widen the result. Defaults and bounds are enforced on the server even when the UI also enforces them.
items.applicability: Applies to every parameterized report and to any report-run API that accepts user-supplied filter values.
items.example: A 'branch' parameter is an enum bound as `WHERE branch_id = @branchId`; supplying `branchId='5 OR 1=1'` fails type validation and never reaches the query. A date-range parameter rejects start > end and caps the span at 366 days.
items.limitation: Tight parameter typing constrains exploratory ad-hoc reporting; genuinely free-form analysis should use a separate, sandboxed query tool rather than relaxing parameter validation.

items.title: Report definitions are immutably versioned with explicit published revisions
items.description: Each report definition (template + dataset binding + parameter schema) is versioned as an immutable revision rather than mutated in place. Editing produces a new draft revision; publishing promotes a specific revision to 'current'. A report run records the exact revision it executed, so a regenerated or re-exported report is reproducible and auditable. Version numbers should be monotonic and combine a human-readable semantic version with an internal revision id, and prior published versions remain retrievable for re-run and comparison.
items.applicability: Applies to any platform where reports change over time and historical reproducibility or rollback is required.
items.example: Report R-204 at v3 (revision 4471) is current; an edit creates draft v4 (revision 4490). A run executed yesterday is stamped revision 4471, so re-running it next year still uses the v3 layout and query even though v4 is now published.
items.limitation: Immutable revisions accumulate storage; a retention policy (e.g., keep all published, prune superseded drafts after N days) is needed to bound growth.

items.title: Report changes require approval before a revision can be published
items.description: A new or edited report revision enters a Draft -> PendingApproval -> Approved/Rejected lifecycle, and only an Approved revision may become 'current' and be runnable by general users. Approval is performed by a role distinct from the author (separation of duties), and the approver reviews the diff against the prior published revision, the dataset binding, and the parameter schema. The transition records approver identity, timestamp, and an optional comment, and rejection returns the revision to Draft with feedback. Reports that touch sensitive datasets may require dual approval. This mirrors the platform's curated-knowledge approval model: nothing untrusted reaches consumers without an explicit human gate.
items.applicability: Applies to any report whose output is distributed, scheduled, or used for operational or regulatory decisions.
items.example: An author edits the reconciliation template to add a 'customer_email' column and submits for approval; the approver sees the diff flags a new PII column, requests data-protection sign-off, and only then approves, moving the revision to current.
items.limitation: Mandatory approval slows iteration; teams often allow a lighter or auto-approve path for sandbox/personal reports that are never shared or scheduled.

items.title: Export controls gate format, volume, and PII visibility per role
items.description: Export is a privileged operation, not a free side effect of viewing a report. Authorization checks who may export, in which formats (an on-screen viewer may be permitted while CSV/XLSX bulk export is restricted), and whether PII or restricted columns are included, masked, or redacted in the exported artifact. Row-volume caps and rate limits prevent bulk exfiltration, and every export records the user, report revision, parameter values, row count, format, and column set actually emitted. Sensitive exports can require justification or step-up authentication, and exported files should be watermarked or tagged with a classification label.
items.applicability: Applies to all export paths (download, email delivery, scheduled distribution, API) for any report that can surface restricted data.
items.example: An analyst can view masked account numbers on screen but the CSV export of the same report omits the column unless the user holds the 'export-pii' role; a 5M-row attempt is blocked by the 250k export cap and logged.
items.limitation: Column masking and row caps can break downstream consumers that expected full data; export entitlements must be coordinated with legitimate operational needs.

items.title: Scheduled reports run as governed jobs with bound parameters and pinned revisions
items.description: A schedule binds a specific report revision, a fixed set of parameter values (or a deterministic rule such as 'previous business day'), a recipient/delivery target, and a cron-style cadence, executing under a defined service or owner identity whose entitlements gate the data and export rights. Scheduled runs must be idempotent and overlap-safe (skip or queue if the prior run is still executing), apply timeouts, and record success/failure with the same run audit as interactive runs. Failures alert the owner and retry under a bounded policy rather than silently dropping a delivery. Because a schedule outlives its author, deactivating or offboarding the owner must trigger re-ownership or disablement so orphaned schedules do not keep delivering sensitive data.
items.applicability: Applies to recurring distribution of reports by email, file drop, or API push without a human in the loop.
items.example: Schedule S-77 runs R-204 revision 4471 every business day at 06:00 with parameter as-of = T-1, owner = svc-reporting, delivers PDF to a distribution list; if the 06:00 run is still going at 06:00 next day it is skipped and an alert is raised.
items.limitation: Pinning a revision means a schedule keeps using an old layout until explicitly repointed; teams must reconcile schedules when a report is re-published.

items.title: Report data lineage traces every field from source to rendered cell
items.description: Each report should be able to answer, for any output column, where the value came from: the source tables/views, the dataset (query) that read them, the parameters that filtered them, and the template expression that computed the displayed value. Capturing lineage at the dataset and template level (rather than per-row) lets governance assess impact when a source schema changes and lets consumers trust a figure by inspecting its derivation. Lineage metadata is recorded with the report definition and stamped onto each run, including the as-of/snapshot time of the underlying data so a number is interpretable against the right point in time. This supports impact analysis ('which reports break if column X is dropped') and regulatory traceability.
items.applicability: Applies to regulated, financial, or operational reporting where figures must be defensible and source-change impact must be assessable.
items.example: Report column 'Net Exposure' resolves to template expr SUM([gross]) - SUM([collateral]) over dataset DS_EXPOSURE, sourced from tables LEDGER.POSITIONS and RISK.COLLATERAL as of the 22:00 batch snapshot; dropping COLLATERAL.value flags this report in impact analysis.
items.limitation: Maintaining accurate column-level lineage is costly and can drift if datasets use complex SQL; lineage is often reliable at table/dataset granularity but approximate at the individual-column level.

items.title: Report runs are recorded in an append-only audit trail
items.description: Every report execution writes an immutable audit record capturing who ran it, when, the report id and exact revision, the resolved parameter values, the data as-of/snapshot time, row count, render target, outcome (success/failure/timeout), and duration. Export and delivery events are audited as distinct actions linked to the run. The audit store is append-only with no in-place updates or deletes, and access to the audit itself is restricted and logged. This run-level audit, combined with the definition change history, lets an investigator reconstruct exactly what a given user saw at a given time and why.
items.applicability: Applies to all interactive and scheduled report executions and exports, especially over regulated or sensitive data.
items.example: Audit row: user=jsmith, report=R-204 rev=4471, params={asOf:2026-06-19, branch:ALL}, snapshot=2026-06-19T22:00Z, rows=18342, target=PDF, exported=true, status=Success, ms=842 — written once, never mutated.
items.limitation: High-volume reporting produces large audit datasets requiring partitioning, retention, and archival; audit must capture parameters without storing the report's actual sensitive output values.

items.title: Report change history records definition diffs across revisions
items.description: Distinct from the run audit, change history tracks how a report definition evolved: each revision stores the author, timestamp, the prior revision it derived from, the approval outcome, and a structured diff of what changed (added/removed columns, formula edits, parameter schema changes, dataset re-binding). A reviewer or auditor can view a side-by-side comparison of any two revisions and understand the rationale via a required change comment. Sensitive transitions — adding a PII column, widening a parameter's allowed set, or changing the source dataset — should be highlighted in the diff to focus approval. Change history feeds rollback (re-publish a prior revision) and post-incident review.
items.applicability: Applies to any governed report whose layout, query, or parameters can be edited over its lifetime.
items.example: Comparing R-204 v3 vs v4 shows: +column customer_email (PII), formula 'total' changed from SUM([amount]) to SUM([net_amount]), author=alee, comment='align to net per finance', approval=pending — the PII addition is flagged red in the diff.
items.limitation: Structured diffing is straightforward for declarative templates but harder when datasets embed complex hand-written SQL, where the diff may show the whole query rather than a semantic change.
